blurb: Plump, egg-shaped leaves dusted in a chalky lavender-blue farina — like a handful of sugared almonds. That powdery coating is its sunscreen, so try not to rub it off.
size: Loose rosettes of egg-shaped leaves ~4–5 in tall

care.light: Bright light with a few hours of gentle direct sun keeps the leaves fat and the pastel colours strong; too little and it stretches and greens up.
care.water: Classic soak-and-dry — a deep drink only once the leaves feel slightly soft and the soil is bone dry. Water the soil, never over the powdery leaves.
care.soil: A fast-draining gritty mix; those water-filled leaves rot quickly in anything that stays damp.
care.temperature: Comfortable at room temperature; keep it above freezing and bring it in well before frost.
care.dormancy: Grows mainly in the milder parts of the year; ease off water in the hottest peak of summer and in deep winter.
care.feeding: A dilute, low-nitrogen feed every 6–8 weeks through spring and summer is plenty.
care.potting: Snug pots are fine; repot every couple of years. Handle by the stem — fingerprints in the farina are permanent until the leaf is replaced.
care.propagation: Very easy from a gently twisted-off leaf laid on dry mix, and from offsets.
care.bloom: Arching stalks of red-orange bell flowers peeking from between colourful bracts in late winter to spring.
care.troubles: Overwatering (translucent, mushy leaves) is the main risk; mealybugs hide between the tight leaves.
care.toxicity: Pachyphytum isn't listed individually by the ASPCA, but it's a Crassulaceae relative of the pet-safe echeverias — considered non-toxic and safe around cats and dogs.

sources.title: Blue Echeveria (Echeveria glauca), a pet-safe Crassulaceae — Toxic and Non-Toxic Plants
